title: Radioactivity — alpha, beta and gamma radiation
examTip: Nuclear equations must balance on both sides — total mass number on left = total mass number on right, and same for atomic number. Always write the atomic and mass numbers for every particle including the radiation emitted.
introTips: Radioactive decay is spontaneous — it is not triggered by anything external like heat or pressure; it just happens randomly.
The three types of radiation have very different penetrating powers and ionising abilities — these go in opposite directions.
Alpha is the most ionising but the least penetrating; gamma is the least ionising but the most penetrating.
Nuclear equations use standard notation: mass number on top, atomic number on bottom, element symbol in the middle.
Background radiation is always present and comes from natural sources — you are exposed to low levels every day.
intro: Radioactivity was discovered almost by accident — Henri Becquerel left uranium salts on top of a photographic plate in 1896 and found the plate had been exposed even in the dark. What was coming out of the uranium? It turned out to be three completely different types of radiation — each with very different properties, very different dangers, and (eventually) very different uses. Understanding radioactivity has given us cancer treatments, smoke detectors, carbon dating, and nuclear power. It has also caused some of history's worst accidents. Knowing the difference between the three types is the essential starting point.

keyPoints.heading: Alpha radiation (α)
keyPoints.content: Alpha (α) radiation consists of a helium nucleus: 2 protons and 2 neutrons (mass number 4, atomic number 2). When a nucleus emits an alpha particle, it loses 2 protons and 2 neutrons — the mass number decreases by 4 and the atomic number decreases by 2, forming a new element. Nuclear equation notation: ⁴₂He. Properties: stopped by a few centimetres of air or a sheet of paper. Highly ionising — each alpha particle knocks many electrons off atoms as it travels, creating lots of ion pairs. Short range in air (~5 cm). Relatively slow-moving and massive compared to other radiation. Danger: very dangerous inside the body (inhaled or ingested) because of high ionisation — but outside the body, skin stops it easily. Example nuclear equation: radium-226 decays by alpha emission → radon-222: ²²⁶₈₈Ra → ²²²₈₆Rn + ⁴₂He.
writeBullets: Alpha particle — 2 protons + 2 neutrons (helium nucleus); mass number 4, atomic number 2
Mass number decreases by 4, atomic number decreases by 2 after alpha emission
Stopped by paper or a few cm of air — least penetrating
Highly ionising — creates many ion pairs per cm of path
Short range in air (~5 cm); most dangerous if ingested or inhaled

keyTerms.def: A helium nucleus (2 protons + 2 neutrons); emitted during alpha decay; highly ionising, stopped by paper
Radiation that knocks electrons off atoms, creating ions — alpha is the most ionising of the three types

keyPoints.memTip: Alpha = helium nucleus (⁴₂He). Loses 4 from mass, 2 from atomic number. Stopped by paper. Very ionising.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Mabel should practise writing out alpha decay equations from scratch — filling in the daughter nucleus numbers by subtraction.

keyPoints.heading: Beta radiation (β)
keyPoints.content: Beta (β) radiation is a fast-moving electron emitted from the nucleus. A neutron in the nucleus converts into a proton plus an electron — the electron is emitted as beta radiation. The nucleus gains one proton (atomic number increases by 1) and the mass number stays the same (a neutron became a proton, same mass). Nuclear notation: ⁰₋₁e (mass number 0, atomic number −1). Properties: stopped by a few mm of aluminium (or about 1 m of air). Moderately ionising — less ionising than alpha, more than gamma. Example nuclear equation: carbon-14 decays by beta emission → nitrogen-14: ¹⁴₆C → ¹⁴₇N + ⁰₋₁e. Note: a neutrino is also emitted but you do not need to include this at GCSE. Beta radiation is faster and lighter than alpha, so it penetrates further but ionises less.
writeBullets: Beta particle — fast electron from the nucleus; neutron → proton + electron (+ neutrino)
Mass number unchanged; atomic number increases by 1 after beta emission
Stopped by a few mm of aluminium or ~1 m of air
Moderately ionising — less than alpha, more than gamma
Beta notation in equations: ⁰₋₁e

keyTerms.def: A fast electron emitted from the nucleus when a neutron converts to a proton; stopped by thin aluminium
A neutron in the nucleus converts to a proton, emitting a beta particle (electron) — atomic number increases by 1

keyPoints.memTip: Beta = fast electron from nucleus. Mass stays same, atomic number goes up by 1. Stopped by aluminium.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Common confusion: beta particles come FROM the nucleus — the nucleus does not already contain electrons, it creates them during the decay.

keyPoints.heading: Gamma radiation (γ)
keyPoints.content: Gamma (γ) radiation is electromagnetic radiation — high-frequency, high-energy waves (part of the EM spectrum, beyond X-rays). It has no mass and no charge. Gamma is often emitted after alpha or beta decay when the nucleus is still in an excited state and releases excess energy as a gamma ray. The nucleus does not change element when it emits gamma — mass number and atomic number are both unchanged. Nuclear notation: ⁰₀γ. Properties: never fully stopped but significantly reduced by thick lead (several cm) or thick concrete (metres). Weakly ionising — travels a long distance through air and tissue, ionising far fewer atoms per cm than alpha or beta. Danger: can penetrate the body and damage internal organs and DNA from outside the body — but ionises less densely than alpha.
writeBullets: Gamma ray — electromagnetic radiation; no mass, no charge
Mass number and atomic number both unchanged after gamma emission
Reduced (not fully stopped) by thick lead or thick concrete
Weakly ionising — travels far, few ion pairs per cm
Often emitted alongside alpha or beta as the nucleus loses excess energy

keyTerms.def: High-energy electromagnetic radiation emitted from the nucleus; no mass or charge; weakly ionising but very penetrating
Waves that travel at the speed of light; gamma rays are the highest energy part of the EM spectrum

keyPoints.memTip: Gamma = EM wave. No mass, no charge. Weakly ionising. Penetrates everything — only reduced (not stopped) by lead.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Emphasise that 'stopped by lead' is not quite right — gamma is only significantly reduced, not completely blocked.

keyPoints.heading: Background radiation and nuclear equations
keyPoints.content: Background radiation is low-level radiation always present in the environment from natural and artificial sources. Natural sources (~85% in the UK): radon gas from rocks (largest source), cosmic rays from space, food and drink (potassium-40), rocks and buildings. Artificial sources (~15%): medical uses (X-rays, radiotherapy), nuclear power stations (small), nuclear weapons testing (historical). Writing nuclear equations: the key rule is that both mass numbers and atomic numbers must balance on each side. Alpha emission example: ²²⁶₈₈Ra → ²²²₈₆Rn + ⁴₂He. Beta emission example: ¹⁴₆C → ¹⁴₇N + ⁰₋₁e. Gamma emission: no change to mass or atomic numbers. Steps: (1) write the parent nucleus; (2) write the radiation with its numbers; (3) balance the remaining numbers to find the daughter nucleus.
writeBullets: Background radiation — always present; mainly from radon gas, cosmic rays, and food/drink
Nuclear equations: mass numbers must balance; atomic numbers must balance
Alpha: mass number −4, atomic number −2 in daughter nucleus
Beta: mass number unchanged, atomic number +1 in daughter nucleus
Gamma: no change to mass or atomic number — nucleus loses energy only

keyTerms.def: Low-level radiation from natural and artificial sources always present in the environment
An equation showing radioactive decay with mass numbers and atomic numbers balanced on both sides

keyPoints.memTip: Balance top numbers, balance bottom numbers. Radon is the biggest source of background radiation in the UK.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Give Mabel practice completing nuclear equations where the daughter nucleus is unknown — this is the most common calculation in this topic.

tables.title: Comparison of alpha, beta and gamma radiation
rows: Reduced by thick lead or concrete
No change to Z or A

0.question: An alpha particle consists of which particles?
0.correctFeedback: Correct! An alpha particle is a helium nucleus: 2 protons + 2 neutrons, giving mass number 4 and atomic number 2.
0.wrongFeedback: An alpha particle is a helium nucleus: 2 protons and 2 neutrons. Mass number = 4, atomic number = 2. It has no electrons.

1.question: Which type of radiation is stopped by a few millimetres of aluminium?
1.correctFeedback: Correct! Beta radiation (fast electrons) is stopped by a few mm of aluminium. Alpha is stopped by paper; gamma requires thick lead to be significantly reduced.
1.wrongFeedback: Beta radiation is stopped by a few mm of aluminium. Alpha is stopped by paper (or a few cm of air); gamma is only significantly reduced by thick lead or concrete.

3.question: Radon-222 (²²²₈₆Rn) decays by alpha emission. What is the atomic number of the daughter nucleus?
3.correctFeedback: Correct! Alpha emission: atomic number decreases by 2. 86 − 2 = 84. The daughter nucleus is polonium-218.
3.wrongFeedback: Alpha emission removes 2 protons from the nucleus, so atomic number decreases by 2: 86 − 2 = 84. The mass number also decreases by 4: 222 − 4 = 218.

commonMistakes: Mixing up ionising power and penetrating power — alpha is most ionising but least penetrating; gamma is least ionising but most penetrating. These go in opposite directions.
Saying beta particles come from the electron shells — they are created in the nucleus when a neutron converts to a proton, then emitted.
In nuclear equations, forgetting that atomic numbers must also balance — most students remember mass numbers but forget to check the bottom numbers.
Saying gamma is 'stopped by lead' — it is significantly reduced but not fully blocked. A common phrase is 'absorbed by thick lead'.
Confusing the effect on the nucleus: alpha reduces both mass number (−4) and atomic number (−2); beta increases atomic number (+1) but leaves mass number unchanged; gamma changes neither.
examTips: For nuclear equations: check both the top numbers (mass numbers) and the bottom numbers (atomic numbers) balance on each side — this earns you both marks.
When comparing types of radiation, link ionising power and penetrating power together — more ionising means it loses energy faster, so it penetrates less far.
Questions about hazards want you to say: inside the body, alpha is most dangerous (high ionisation of nearby cells); outside the body, gamma is most dangerous (penetrates to internal organs).
Radon gas is the largest source of background radiation in the UK — often asked in 'state a source of background radiation' questions.
revisionCardBullets: Alpha: helium nucleus (2p + 2n); mass 4, charge +2; stopped by paper; most ionising
Alpha decay: mass number −4, atomic number −2
Beta: fast electron from nucleus; mass 0, charge −1; stopped by aluminium; moderate ionising
Beta decay: neutron → proton + electron; atomic number +1, mass number unchanged
Gamma: EM wave; no mass, no charge; reduced by thick lead; least ionising, most penetrating
Gamma emission: no change to mass number or atomic number
Nuclear equations: both mass numbers and atomic numbers must balance
Background radiation: radon gas (main source), cosmic rays, food, building materials
Alpha most dangerous inside body; gamma most dangerous from outside body
Ionising power: α > β > γ; Penetrating power: γ > β > α
